brief: GAP/WHY: Takotsubo syndrome (stress cardiomyopathy) = reversible LV systolic dysfunction presenting as acute heart failure, usually triggered by emotional/physical stress; shows transient apical ballooning or regional wall-motion abnormality; mimics and must be distinguished from acute coronary syndrome (ACS). Episodes shortly after pacemaker implantation are reported, BUT recurrence within 3 months of apparent recovery is rare/poorly described. DESIGN/SETTING: single-patient case report; peri-implant / post-pacemaker hospital setting. POPULATION/N: n=1 patient; indication for device = atrioventricular (AV) block. INTERVENTION: dual-chamber pacemaker implantation. COMPARISON: none (case report). KEY EVENTS/RESULTS (no statistical numbers — case report; copy timeline faithfully): (a) acute heart failure developed 1 day after discharge; (b) diagnosed with previously undiagnosed stress cardiomyopathy / Takotsubo at that time; (c) recovered from initial episode; (d) RECURRENCE of stress cardiomyopathy 3 months later; (e) recurrence occurred despite guideline-concordant therapy. Notable = very early procedure-associated Takotsubo with short-interval (3-month) recurrence. MANAGEMENT POINTS: management is supportive; outcomes depend on timely echocardiography, exclusion of ACS, and structured follow-up; obtain early bedside echocardiography + selective coronary evaluation; use obstruction-aware supportive care; continue RAS blockade after discharge; ensure structured follow-up. TAKEAWAY: consider Takotsubo syndrome in peri-implant cardiac decompensation after pacemaker implantation; recognition in peri-implant setting is crucial; recurrence can occur even with guideline-concordant therapy.
